title: Building WAR Predict: Teaching a Model to Think Like a Scout
excerpt: I've loved baseball my whole life. I also love data. WAR Predict is what happens when those two things collide — a Random Forest model trained on a decade of Baseball Reference data to predict player value.
content:
I've loved baseball my whole life. I also love data. WAR Predict is what happens when those two things collide — a Random Forest model trained on a decade of Baseball Reference data to predict player value.

The hardest part wasn't the model. It was deciding which features actually matter. Age is obvious, but age is nonlinear — a 27-year-old and a 33-year-old are not symmetrically positioned on the performance curve. Rolling OBP and SLG over the past two seasons matter more than single-year stats. Injury flags are noisy but real.

I ended up with 9 features, TimeSeriesSplit cross-validation (so I'm never training on the future), and a deployed Flask app on Railway. The next step is adding confidence intervals — because a model that can't express uncertainty is a model that's lying to you.

title: MIPS Calling Conventions Finally Made Sense When I Stopped Memorizing
excerpt: For weeks, $ra, $sp, and the saved registers felt like arbitrary rules handed down from on high. Then I stopped trying to memorize them and started asking why — and everything clicked.
content:
For weeks, $ra, $sp, and the saved registers felt like arbitrary rules handed down from on high. Then I stopped trying to memorize them and started asking why — and everything clicked.

The calling convention exists to answer one question: who is responsible for what? The caller owns $t0–$t9 because they're temporaries — if the callee trashes them, the caller should have saved them. The callee owns $s0–$s7 because if a function promises to preserve them, the caller can trust they'll be the same after the call returns.

$ra is just a register that holds the return address — jal writes to it, jr $ra reads from it. When you nest function calls, you push $ra onto the stack before calling again, then pop it back. The stack is just memory you're managing manually. Once that framing landed, the rest followed.

title: gitlogs: What Your Commit History Says About You
excerpt: I commit a lot. Sometimes at 2am. Sometimes five times in an hour. I wanted to know what patterns were actually there — so I built a tool to find out.
content:
I commit a lot. Sometimes at 2am. Sometimes five times in an hour. I wanted to know what patterns were actually there — so I built gitlogs: a Streamlit app that analyzes your git commit history with AI-powered insights.

The stack is GitPython for reading the repo, pandas for crunching the data, Plotly for charts, and the Anthropic SDK so Claude can surface patterns you wouldn't notice yourself — things like whether your commit messages get vaguer when you're moving fast, or which hours of the day produce your cleanest diffs.

I built it commit-by-commit with conventional commit discipline (feat:, fix:, chore: — each commit independently testable). The scaffold is done. Loader, analyzer, charts, and UI are next. It's the kind of tool I would have wanted six months ago.
